title: Status, Power and Ritual Interaction: A Relational Reading of Durkheim, Goffman, and Collins By Theodore D. Kemper Ashgate. 2011. 322 pages. $124.95 hardback
abstract: How do emotions emerge and change in social contexts? Why some emotions and not others? How are people bonded together as communities or even societies? These are the kinds of questions that Kemper (implicitly or explicitly) asks in this thought-provoking, challenging book. Here, I mean challenging in the literal sense that Kemper is asking us, the readers, to rethink how we do sociology by reimagining the basis for social interaction. He does this through his status-power theory in direct competition with some of the leading contenders in sociological thought on the sorts of questions above. So, the theory itself will not appear new to those who have read Kemper's work before, particularly his brilliant 1978 book A Social Interactional Theory of Emotions. While the word emotions is not in the title of the book reviewed here, it perhaps should be. Though the sociology of emotions has become a rapidly expanding field in recent decades, Kemper's work still remains among the most thoroughly structural and comprehensive, at least from a micro-sociological perspective. It is this level of abstraction that makes the comparison with Goffman and Collins in particular so relevant. Though Kemper plausibly claims that the status-power model applies to macro social relations as well, such as power struggles and status claims between nation-states, this more macro (and especially historical) orientation remains a lacuna in the book.
